coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots functionalization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bin Bin
Liu, Meng Li
Li, Chun Mei
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have been applied in a variety of fields. Currently, functionalization of CDs is an extremely useful method for effectively tuning their intrinsic structure and surface state. Heteroatom doping and surface modification are two functionalization strategies for improving the photophysical performance and broadening the range of applications for fluorescent CDs. Heteroatom doping in CDs can be used to tune their intrinsic properties, which has received significant research interests because of its simplicity. Surface modification can be applied for varying active sites and the functional groups on the CDs surface, which can endow fluorescent CDs with the unique properties resulting from functional ligand. In this review, we summarize the structural and physicochemical properties of functional CDs. We focused our review on the latest developments in functionalization strategies for CDs and discuss the detailed characteristics of different functionalization methods. Ultimately, we hope to inform researchers on the latest progress in functionalization of CDs and provide perspectives on future developments for functionalization of CDs and their potential applications.

The green and red arrows indicate the spin directions. ∑ indicates the spin vortex direction characterized by clockwise spin rotation (∑ = −1) and counterclockwise (∑ = +1). After absorbing the photons, the excited electrons on the conduction bands C∑=+1 and C∑=−1 will quickly relax to C∑=−1 band minimum due to the inelastic phonon scattering. Similarly, the holes will quickly relax to V∑=+1 band maximum. However, the radiative recombination of C∑=−1 → V∑=+1 is a spin-forbidden process due to different spin states. Moreover, the minimum of C∑=−1 band and the maximum of V∑=+1 band are located at different positions in the Brillouin Zone. This leads to an indirect band gap for recombination [186]. ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have been applied in a variety of fields. Currently, functionalization of CDs is an extremely useful method for effectively tuning their intrinsic structure and surface state. Heteroatom doping and surface modification are two functionalization strategies for improving the photophysical performance and broadening the range of applications for fluorescent CDs. Heteroatom doping in CDs can be used to tune their intrinsic properties, which has received significant research interests because of its simplicity. Surface modification can be applied for varying active sites and the functional groups on the CDs surface, which can endow fluorescent CDs with the unique properties resulting from functional ligand. In this review, we summarize the structural and physicochemical properties of functional CDs. We focused our review on the latest developments in functionalization strategies for CDs and discuss the detailed characteristics of different functionalization methods. Ultimately, we hope to inform researchers on the latest progress in functionalization of CDs and provide perspectives on future developments for functionalization of CDs and their potential applications.

We summarize the physicochemical properties and functionalization strategies of CDs in hoping to give a guiding role for improving the photophysical performance and broadening the range of applications.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial and have attracted attention from researchers since they were discovered in 2004 by Scrivens et al. [1] CDs can be classified as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) in the light of their variation in crystal structure and quantum confinement [2]. In contrast to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs exhibit numerous fascinating merits, including tunable fluorescence, nontoxicity, long-term photobleaching resistance, and dispersibility in water, which facilitate their application in chemosensing, biosensing, imaging, cancer therapy, catalysis, solar cells, and light emitting diodes [3–12]. Currently, some review papers about CDs have been published. Gao’ group [13] summarized the synthesis methods and common application fields of CDs. Qian’ group [14] discussed the optical properties of CDs and their synthesis approaches including top-down methods and bottom-up approaches. Furthermore, the authors focus on development of chemosensors and biosensors based on functional CDs. Sun’ group [15] discussed the mechanistic origin of CDs fluorescence and summarized the development of CDs based fluorescent sensors in recent 5 years. Our group systematically summarized the green synthesis of CDs [16], and the application of CDs in fluorescence and chemiluminescence sensing [17]. Functionalization is very important for understanding the intrinsic structure and optical properties of CDs, so it is necessary to summarize the functionalization of CDs.

Non-functionalized CDs usually consist of carbon and oxygen elements resulting from “top-down” cutting or break down of carbon materials, such as carbon fibers, activated carbon, and candle soot through chemical, electrochemical, or physical approaches [18–21]. Bare CDs exhibit low luminescent efficiency (quantum yield (QY) <5%) because epoxy and carboxylic groups on the surface always induce nonradiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs, suppressing pristine emission [22]. Meanwhile, non-functionalized CDs usually emit blue fluorescence and lack diverse active sites due to the unicity of surface functional groups, which limits their applications. As a result, functionalization of CDs is a powerful strategy for improving the photophysical and photochemical performance. Functionalization endows CDs with unique properties, thereby broadening their scope of applications. Currently, functionalization strategies include heteroatom doping and surface modification. Heteroatom-doped CDs can be directly synthesized through a one-step “bottom-up” method involving pyrolysis or carbonization of small molecules containing heteroatoms and other precursors, while surface modified CDs usually go require two or more steps. CDs can be modified with functional ligands via physical mixing or chemical bonding such as electrostatic interaction, NHS/EDC method, and amidation reaction.

Heteroatom doping can be used to efficiently adjust the intrinsic structure and electron distribution in CDs, including non-metal and metal doping. Among the various non-metals, nitrogen doping is a very efficient method for improving the QY of CDs because electrons trapped by the new formed N-state can exhibit high fluorescence yield [23]. In addition, nitrogen doping can be used to tune the fluorescence and determine the excitation independence behaviour due to passivated surface states [24,25]. Other non-metal dopants such as sulfur, phosphorus, silicon, or a combination of different dopants (known as co-doping) have also been proposed for improving the optical properties of CDs. Metal doping can be used to optimize the optical properties by modulating the band structure and impart new functionalities to the doped CDs. Various metal atoms such as copper, gadolinium, and manganese are used to prepare the metal doped CDs. Copper-doped CDs can be strong catalysts because copper atoms act as strong electron acceptors or donors. Moreover, gadolinium doped CDs can be used for fluorescence and magnetic resonance dual-modal bioimaging because gadolinium atoms are the most commonly used T1 contrast agents due to the high r1 relaxivity of gadolinium. Therefore, chemical heteroatom doping serves as an ideal strategy for engineering the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs, but the concurrent increase in toxicity requires attention [26].

Surface modification is a strategy for functionalizing CDs with ions, organic molecules, polymers, DNA, and proteins. Unlike heteroatom doping, surface modification with functional ligands can be used to vary the surface states and increase the number of active sites. L-cysteine modified CDs can be used for long-term in situ imaging of the Golgi apparatus because L-cysteine or its residues can cross link with the Golgi apparatus [27]. CDs modified with molecularly imprinted polymers are used as a biocompatible probe for targeting cancer cells because molecularly imprinted polymers provide specific recognition of glucuronic acid on the surface of certain cancers [28]. Meanwhile, surface modification (i.e., surface passivation) is also a very efficient way to increase the QY of CDs. Polymers or other molecules attach or bind on the surface of CDs to enhance the luminescence efficiency because emission resulting from surface energy traps will become more stable after surface passivation [29].

In this review, we introduce doping and surface modification methods that been used to improve the properties of CDs for various applications. The structural and physicochemical properties of functional CDs are summarized in Section 2. Heteroatom doping is described in detail in Section 3, and surface modification is discussed in Section 4. This review is intended to help researchers understand the influence of functionalization on the intrinsic structure and optical properties of CDs and provide valuable information on methods for controlling the properties of CDs. We hope our work will promote the application of CDs in various nanomaterials and in nanochemistry.

CDs are generally considered a new kind of quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, which were first synthesized by Xu et al. in 2004 [1]. The unclear mechanistic growth of these materials creates a complicated core structure and surface groups, thus there are a handful of sub-classes of CDs. These CDs are all-too-often arbitrarily and haphazardly classified with new names or acronyms, giving the false impression that a new material has been generated [8]. In this review, according to their intrinsic structure, CDs can be simply divided into three categories: amorphous CNDs, crystalline CQDs and GQDs [2] GQDs are single-layer or multi-layer flaky crystalline nanoparticles with conjugated π-electron graphene layers that also exhibit quantum confinement (Fig. 1a and d). CQDs consisting of multiple layers of graphitic sheets are spherical crystalline nanoparticles that exhibit quantum confinement (Fig. 1b and e). CNDs with amorphous structure are considered to consist of aggregated or cross-linked monomers or linear polymers (Fig. 1c and f) [33]. CNDs are not crystalline, thus they lack quantum confinement. Note that some studies show that hexagonal carbon networks also exist in amorphous CNDs. In other words, some CNDs do not completely consist of disordered monomers or linear polymers. Therefore, this establishes graphene fragments as components of CNDs and bridges the gap between graphene and completely amorphous structures [34].

Here, we further compared other members of carbon material family such as carbon nano-onions (CNOs), polymer dots and carbon nanodiamonds. The CNOs consist of quasi-spherical- and polyhedral-shaped graphitic layers which are close to each other [35]. The structure of CNOs contains hexagonal and pentagonal rings with carbon atoms locating at the vertices. Neighbouring carbon atoms can form two single bonds and one double bond with delocalized π-electrons across the molecule [35]. The CNOs had a wide range of sizes from 2 to 200 nm, with synthesis temperatures up to thousands of degrees. In contrast, the size of CDs is usually less than 10 nm, with synthesis temperature usually below 300 °C. The CNOs with large size can be converted into small fluorescent CDs by chemical approaches [36]. Polymer dots are aggregated or cross-linked polymers prepared from linear polymers or monomers by covalent and non-covalent bonds [37], which are similar to the CNDs defined by Valcãrcel. Carbon nanodiamonds materials, including diamond-like carbon, sintered diamond phases and diamond micro- and nanoparticles, have been well-suited for biological applications, such as labelling and drug delivery because of their inertness, small size and surface structure [38]. The structure of nanodiamonds depends strongly on their preparation conditions. Fluorescent carbon nanodiamonds contain negatively charged nitrogen-vacancy centers, (N-V)−, and show unique photophysical features such as extended far-red emission (600–800 nm), long fluorescence lifetimes (ca. 10 ns), and excellent photostability [39].

The fundamental elements in the CDs are carbon and oxygen. In the absence of additional functionalization, bare CDs (i.e., oxidized CDs) generally contain abundant carboxylic groups on their surface [40]. In order to improve the luminescent efficiency and broaden the range of applications for CDs, some metal and non-metal elements have been used when synthesizing functionalized CDs. Nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus are common non-metal dopants. Metal dopants include copper, iron, gadolinium, and lanthanide. As a result, the chemical composition of CDs is strongly dependent on the reaction precursors. Table 1 compared the differences between precursors, doping reagents and QY of different functionalized CDs. N-doping is the most significant factor in improving the luminescence efficiency of CDs. Other non-metal dopants and metal dopants can also increase the QY of CDs in some degree.

Although they belong to CDs family, crystal structures of CNDs are inconsistent with CQDs and GQDs. Regarding CNDs, it is difficult to observe lattice fringes due to their amorphous structure or non-crystalline structures [63]. Regarding CQDs and GQDs, HRTEM images show that these materials are highly crystalline with interplanar distances of ca. 0.21 nm ascribed to the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [64–66], and the spacing of ca. 0.34 nm between graphene layers is attributed to the (002) lattice spacing in graphene [44,67,68]. The spacing between graphene layers (d 002) in CDs is dependent on the carbon precursor, synthetic approach, and heteroatom dopant. Ajayan et al. [19] showed that the d 002 spacing (0.403 nm) in GQDs prepared by chemical oxidation and cutting of carbon fibers was broader than that in the carbon fibers (0.364 nm) because oxygen-related groups were introduced into the GQDs during oxidation, thus increasing the interlayer distance.

Moreover, the introduction of heteroatoms can cause an obvious change in the value of d 002. Bourlinos et al. showed that d 002 = 0.31 nm in boron-doped CDs (B-CDs), which is smaller than that in bulk graphite (0.34 nm) because the π-electron density between graphite planes was depleted, which would decrease the interlayer distance [69,70]. However, Kim’s group [71] showed that d 002 = 0.42 nm in B-CDs, which is greater than that in bulk graphite and lacks a clear explanation. Lu’s group [72] showed that an enlarged d 002 value in B-CDs (0.39 nm) may be attributed to the introduction of nitrogen, boron, and oxygen-related groups. In addition to the value of d 002, Kim’s group [73] found that the larger d 100 = 0.24 nm in sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) is attributed to the doping of sulfur atoms. Table 2 compared the differences between type of carbon, doping atoms, precursors, lattice spacing and possible explanation. In general, the doping of heteroatoms may influence the crystal structure of CDs.

The crystal phase of the CDs can be confirmed from X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. CDs usually show a broad diffraction peak from 15° to 45° centered at ca. 23°. This broad peak is due to the small size of CDs and shift to lower angles via oxidation [74,75]. Gu’s group [76] showed that the XRD pattern of Mn doped CDs (Mn-CDs) exhibit a peak centered at 22°, which is attributed to the turbostratic graphitic carbon phase. Moreover, the broad and noisy features indicate Mn-CDs are amorphous. Wang et al. [77] proposed that the low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 25.8° is due to the amorphous structure of CDs. Rogach’s group [78] found that the relatively sharp peak near 27.5° corresponds to a layer spacing of 0.32 nm in the graphite-like multilayer structure. The broad, weak component near 26.8° may be associated with slightly disordered layers in CDs.

The Raman spectrum can be used to analyze the crystallinity of CDs. CDs usually show two characteristic peaks at ca. 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band arises from defects and disordered sp3-hybridized carbon in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon, while the G band is derived from vibrations of ordered sp2-hybridized carbon in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [83–86]. Zhu’s group [87] found that the sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs (S, N-CDs) with such a low carbon content in the lattice only exhibit a D band near 1386 cm−1. Zhang’s group [48] demonstrated that the organosilane-doped CDs (Si-CDs) with amorphous nature only exhibit a weak G band at 1557 cm−1. Therefore, the intensity ratio (I D/I G) of the D band (disorder) to the G band (crystalline) is often used to determine the crystallinity of the carbon core. A large I D/I G value indicates growing structural defects or an increasing amount of disordered carbon within CDs, while a small I D/I G value reveals a highly crystalline CDs core. An I D/I G value of ca. 0.7 is typical of graphite [84,88,89]. Ajayan’s group [90] showed that I D/I G = 1.05 in nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) due to N-doping defects and exposed edge sites. Shen’s group [91] showed that I D/I G = 0.78 in Cu and N co-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs), which indicates that the structure of Cu,N-CDs is graphite-like with a few disordered carbon structures. Tang’s group [74] found that the low I D/I G = 0.115 in potassium doped GQDs (K-GQDs), demonstrating that the structure of K-GQDs is not dominated by internal defects and have a low degree of disorder.

Heteroatom doping can affect the location and intensity of peaks in the Raman spectrum. Rogach’s group [78] found that the blue-shifted G band at 1649 cm−1 compared to the typical in-plane vibration (G band, 1580 cm−1) of carbon atoms is ascribed to the presence of high N and O doping. Chen’s group [79] showed that I D/I G = 1.02 in europium-decorated GQDs (Eu-GQDs) compared to I D/I G = 0.99 in undecorated GQDs. The larger I D/I G value in Eu-GQDs arises because Eu atoms break the hexagonal symmetry of the graphene layers.

CDs contain various allowed electronic transitions, including σ→σ*, σ→π*, π→π*, n→π*, and n→σ*, which are closely relevant to the chemical structure and optical properties of CDs [92]. Aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon core are responsible for the π-states in the CDs. Li’s group [93] showed that the energy gap between the π-states in GQDs gradually decreases as the number of conjugated carbon atoms increases. Functionalization hardly affects the band gap in GQDs, but it can be used to vary their redox potentials by covalently attaching electron-withdrawing or -donating groups. The n-states in CDs can be ascribed to functional groups on the surface of CDs containing lone electron pairs, such as carbonyls and amines. Liang’s group [94] found that oxygen-based and amine-based groups can modify the energy levels of n-states, producing multicolored emission due to the radiation of the excited electrons from n–π* transitions in C = O and C = N groups. In addition, electronic transitions from n-states in the functional groups to the π*-states in the sp2 carbon domains can occur when the functional groups are sp2-bonded carbon. Therefore, the electronic structures and related electronic transitions significantly affect the optical properties of CDs. However, due to the complexity and uncertainty of components in CDs, their electronic structure is still a question that requires further study.

Regarding amorphous CNDs, Clark’s group [34] showed that the electronic structure depends most strongly on the geometry and less so on the atomic hybridization and heteroatom dopants. CNDs showed facet-dependent electronic properties, i.e., there is an inhomogeneous charge distribution on the surface of CNDs (Fig. 2 ). CNDs still exhibit a broad distribution of electronic states, even if the elemental composition is homogeneous. Moreover, the particle size (i.e. quantum confinement) can also influence the electronic structure of CNDs, especially in small particles (distance <1.5 nm). Meanwhile, the appearance of sub-band gap surface states allowed optical transitions in the near-UV and visible range, producing positively and negatively charged sites at the surface.

Regarding GQDs, the electronic structure depends strongly on their shapes and edges [95]. Ritter et al. [96] showed that the metallic behavior of zigzag-edge GQDs is attributed to the presence of zigzag edge states, and the fraction of zigzag edges influences their energy gap. Meanwhile, the electronic structure of GQDs depends sensitively on the crystallographic orientation of their edges. Choi’s group [97] showed that the strong fluorescence from GQDs can arise due to controlled modification of the electronic transitions in nanometer-sized systems, i.e., by size and size-dependent shape/edge-state variations in GQDs. The high-energy fluorescence of GQDs profits from the unique properties of graphene: fast carrier-carrier scattering dominates over electron-phonon scattering. This is beneficial to direct recombination of excited electron-hole pairs, yielding high-energy fluorescence before thermalization of carriers with the lattice.

Compared with semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent dyes, CDs exhibit superior photostability [98,99]. Generally, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains nearly constant under continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp for several hours, whereas organic dyes photo-bleach within minutes [100].

In fact, different types of CDs show different photostability behavior. Our group [32] showed that GQDs exhibit better photostability than CNDs (Fig. 3a). This is because amorphous CNDs can be broken down into linear polymers or monomers during photobleaching (Fig. 3b–d). Moreover, the oxygen content in CNDs increases as the exposure time increases, indicating that fluorescent photobleaching is irrelevant to photo-oxidation. Photo-oxidation of CNDs decreases the radiative recombination rate and increases the nonradiative recombination rate, thus fluorescence efficiency of CNDs reduces. Peukert’s group [33] further showed that the extent of photobleaching is strongly dependent on the duration and intensity of exposure to light and the concentration of CNDs. A lower concentration leads to easier fluorescence bleaching of CNDs due to two reasons: (i) light screening is effective at higher concentrations; (ii) energy dissipation in different CNDs at high concentration further dampens bleaching.

The GQDs exhibit self-assembly behavior in the aqueous medium. It has been proved that solution pH can affect the conformation of single-layer graphene oxide (SLGO) [101]. The SLGO agglomerates as flat sheets at low pH and condenses at high pH enveloping nanoparticle. The pH driven conformational changes of SLGO will directly influence their chemical reactivity and mechanical properties. In view of this, the cluster form of GQDs may attribute to solvent-assisted self-assembly, weak electrostatic interactions, and affinity between surface functional groups similar in this case [102]. Furthermore, Srivastava’ group [103] demonstrated that “self-assembled” GQDs units resulting from solvent stabilization is driven by electrostatic interactions between superficial functional groups of nanomaterial and solvent.

Heteroatom doping is a powerful approach for adjusting the intrinsic structure of CDs, including doping with non-metallic and metallic elements. The introduction of heteroatoms can change the surface structure and electron distribution in CDs, which is related to the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO, thus affecting their fluorescent properties. The fluorescence efficiency of CDs can increase and the emission peak can change by tuning the amounts and types of heteroatom dopants. Generally speaking, non-metallic elements dopants, e.g., nitrogen or silicon, can be used to significantly improve the QY in CDs, while metallic dopants, e.g., copper and manganese, can be used to modulate the band structure of CDs. In addition, metallic dopants can prevent overconsumption of amino and carboxyl groups in the precursors (e.g., citric acid) during dehydration and carbonization via chelation between metal ions and chemical groups [61,104]. Therefore, heteroatom doping is a common functionalization strategy that provides an attractive method for exploiting new performance towards various applications.

Nitrogen can act as n-type impurities that provide excess electrons, which raises the Fermi level and changes the optical properties [92]. Common nitrogen sources are ethylenediamine [41], ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid [94], urea [105], and ammonia [106]. Except for these compounds, some natural products with high nitrogen content are also used as nitrogen sources for preparing nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), such as silk, [107] milk, [108] wool, [109] and silkworm chrysalis [110].

Nitrogen doping can be used to significantly improve the fluorescence QY of CDs through surface passivation. The introduction of nitrogen can produce a new surface state (the so-called N-state) that traps electrons, increases radiative recombination, and suppresses nonradiative recombination (Fig. 4 ) [23,111]. Meanwhile, amino-groups act as electron donors that can improve conjugation of amino-groups-passivated CDs, thus indirectly increasing the QY due to the increased probability of an electronic transition from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state [24]. In addition, a highly stable excited state can be attributed to the high QY in CDs [41]. Until now, the highest reported QY in N-CDs was 94.5% [112].

Meanwhile, the emission properties from CDs can be changed by adding nitrogen on the surface. The undoped CDs usually show broad and excitation-dependent emission behavior because oxygen-related groups (e.g., C-O, C = O and O = C-OH) can produce various surface states (the so-called O-state) (Fig. 4) corresponding to different energy levels below the HOMO, thus inducing different transition modes and a broad UV-vis absorption band [23]. After surface passivation via nitrogen doping, the emission can only occur via radiative transition of sp2 carbon, leading to excitation-independent fluorescence due to a switch from multiple transition modes to a single transition mode (Fig. 5 ) [24].

In addition, nitrogen doping can cause a red-shift in the absorption peak of CDs due to the enhanced electron cloud activity, which narrows the energy gap [24]. Furthermore, nitrogen doping can be used to tune fluorescence from CDs by introducing a new transition model or emission centers. Therefore, nitrogen doping is the most effective approach for varying the fluorescence properties of CDs. The surface of N-CDs is rich in N-containing groups (e.g., N−H group), resulting in a special response towards heavy metal ions (e.g., Au3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+) through reduction or coordination. Therefore, the N-CDs can act as the nanoprobes for fluorescent sensing of various metal ions [84,113,114]. Qian’ group [115] prepared N-doped CQDs by a one-pot solvothermal synthesis and demonstrated that the introduction of nitrogen atoms largely increased the QY of CQDs. Moreover, the N-doped CQDs can act as a multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform for detecting Ag+ and Fe3+ ions based on their distinctive fluorescence responses. Zhou et al. [84] prepared water-soluble N-CDs that exhibited highly sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching toward Au3+ ion by reducing Au3+ ion to neutral Au particles. Therefore, fluorescence resonance energy transfer can occur between N-CDs and Au particles, resulting in fluorescence quenching of N-CDs.

Doping with sulfur can be used to optimize the electronic structure of CDs because the addition of sulfur could provide the density of states or create emissive trap states for photoexcited electrons, which modifies the band-gap energy [116]. The most common sulfur sources, including pyritinol hydrochloride [117], 3-mercaptopropionic acid [118], sodium sulphide [45], ammonium thiocyanate [119], thiourea [120], glutathione [121,122], methionine [123], heparin sodium [116], cysteamine hydrochloride [124], thiomalic acid [125], sulfuric acid [44,87,126,127], sodium thiosulfate [128], mercaptoacetic acid [129], and L-cysteine [130–132] as well as some biomass materials such as durian flesh [133] and garlic, [134] have been used to prepare sulfur-doped CDs (S-CDs).

Sulfur doping can be used to improve the optical and chemical stabilities, as well as the QY in CDs. The introduction of sulfur can eliminate the O-states and enhance the N-state, thus the original surface states have negligible influence in nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) (Fig. 4) [23]. In other words, sulfur can be used to enhance the effects induced by nitrogen in N-CDs via a cooperative effect. Therefore, the N, S-CDs exhibit high QY and excitation-independent emission. Xu et al. [128] prepared S-CDs with blue fluorescence using a facile hydrothermal route. Increased sulphur doping can obviously increase the QY of S-CDs because heteroatoms passivate surface defects and serve as excitation energy traps, thus influencing the fluorescence from S-CDs [135] In particular, sulfur atoms in S-CDs can as catalysts for redox reactions [136], introducing more passivated surface defects in S-CDs that further enhance the fluorescence.

Sulfur doping can efficiently adjust the fluorescent emission from CDs. Ding’s group [133] prepared sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) with controllable lattice by hydrothermal treatment of durian. Sulfur doping can cause a significant red-shift in the emission from GQDs due to charge-injection; this arises because sulfur lattice substitutions have low electronegativity [137]. Moreover, S-GQDs showed a slight excitation-dependent behavior dominated by substitution of sulfur atoms in the lattice in S-GQDs and n−π* transitions [137]. Interestingly, the emission is red-shifted as the dopant concentration increases. Because sulfur atoms in the lattice influence the total number of oxygen-related groups, which can be used to adjust the fluorescence of S-GQDs [138], the excitation wavelength could be easily tuned by selecting the appropriate sulfur concentrations. In addition, the dependence of QY on the excitation wavelength can be tuned by varying the dopant concentrations as this dependence is relevant to the n−π* transition process [139].

S-CDs also have a high affinity towards some metal ions, such as Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ag+ ions through coordination. Tang et al. [126] obtained S-CQDs by hydrothermal carbonization of sulfuric acid, which was used as the sulfur dopant. S-CQDs have high affinity towards Fe3+ ion due to the low electronegativity of sulfur atoms on the surface of S-CQDs, and thus electron-deficient Fe3+ has a higher binding ability towards S-CQDs with electron-rich carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. This destroys the initial electron transition in S-CQDs, while it is beneficial for electron transfer from S-CQDs to Fe3+. The resulting nonradiative electron/hole recombination directly quenches the fluorescence from S-CQDs. Furthermore, Qian’ group [140] developed a fluorometric assay of butyrylcholinesterase through thiol-functionalized CQDs (thiol-CQDs). The fluorescence of thiol-CQDs can be quenched in the presence of hydrogen peroxide owing to the formation of non-fluorescent thiol-CQDs assemblies. The fluorescence can be reversibly recovered by breaking of disulfide bonds caused by external redox stimuli. The specific thiol-triggered disaggregation-induced emission can be well used for the fluorescence turn-on sensing of butyrylcholinesterase.

Phosphorus, located in group 15 of the periodic table, is larger than carbon. Therefore, phosphorus atoms can form substitutional defects in the carbon cluster and behave as n-type donors, thereby modifying the electronic and optical properties [42,92]. Several common phosphorus sources have been used to synthesize phosphorus doped CDs (P-CDs), including phosphorous tribromide, [42] phosphoric acid, [43,141–143] monosodium phosphate, [144] triphenylphosphine, [145] adenosine 5’-triphosphate, [77,146] adenosine 5’-monophosphate, [147] and di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate [148].

Phosphorus doping can enhance the luminescence efficiency of CDs, but this mechanism is still controversial. Feng’s group [42] prepared P-CDs through a solvothermal method with QY reaching 25%. The high QY may be due to the coexistence of defect sites and isolated sp2 carbon clusters, which increase the bandgap into the UV-vis region and produces stronger fluorescence than individual sp2 carbon clusters. The fluorescence enhancement may also arise from isolated sp2 carbon clusters because the introduction of phosphorus into CDs produces more isolated sp2 carbon clusters, and the number of these clusters increases along with increasing phosphorus content. Moreover, the possibility that the formed defects produce fluorescence and that more defects intensify fluorescence cannot be eliminated. Very recently, Haynes’ group [149] argued that phosphorus doping has no obvious effect on the fluorescent properties, including QY and fluorescence lifetime. However, phosphorus doping can significantly improve the photostability of CDs.

Meanwhile, the addition of phosphorus in CDs can change their fluorescence. Das’ group [144] found that phosphorus doping can produce a red-shift in the emission wavelength by preparing three kinds of P-CDs. Furthermore, Chang’s group [150] explained the red-shift in detail by relating it phosphorus doping. P-related groups can introduce a new energy-level above the HOMO of the CDs (the so-called P-state) as these groups act as strong electron donors (Fig. 6 ). Therefore, dual fluorescent emission (R1 and R2) from P and N co-doped CDs (P, N-CDs) is ascribed to the existence of grafted N and P states in CDs, i.e., transitions between the N and P states produces R2 and R1 emission simultaneously (Fig. 6).

In addition, P-CDs exhibit strong binding with some metal ions through coordination, such as Fe3+. Wang et al. [77] obtained P, N-CDs through hydrothermal treatment by using adenosine 5-triphosphate as the phosphorus source. Fe3+ can specifically quench fluorescence from P, N-CDs via static quenching due to the formation of a nonfluorescent complex between Fe3+ and P, N-CDs through Fe−O−P bonds. The linear range of Fe3+ was from 1 to 150 μM, with a detection limit of 0.33 μM. Moreover, Qian’ group [151] prepared phenylboronic acid modified CQDs for α-glucosidase activity monitoring. The fluorescence of CQDs probe was quenched after binding to p-nitrophenol to form a non-fluorescent conjugate by B-O bonds, which can be recovered by switching of the pH from 4 to 8. The switching off the fluorescence of CQDs in the reaction was successfully used for monitoring α-glucosidase activity.

Boron doping has been widely studied. As with other non-metallic elements (N, P, and S), boron doping can be used to change the intrinsic properties of CDs. Meanwhile, the introduction of less electronegative boron atoms accommodates the charge density of the C and N bonding matrix, causing a change in the bonding patterns that subsequently decreases the number of surface states [152]. Common boron sources are borax, [153] boric acid, [69,72,152,154–157] boron trichloride, [47] and 3-formyl-phenylboronic acid [158].

Boron doping can be used to tune the optical properties and enhance the luminescence efficiency of CDs. Feng et al. [47] prepared B-doped CQDs (B-CQDs) via one-pot solvothermal carbonization of BBr3 and hydroquinone. Boron doping in CQDs results in a blue-shift in the emission wavelength due to the strong electron-withdrawing ability of boron. Moreover, the QY (14.8%) of B-CQDs is higher than that of B-free CQDs (3.4%) because boron can serve as an engine for charge transfer, particularly in the excited state. Furthermore, Kim et al. [71] prepared boron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs) via one-step microwave synthesis. These BN-CDs displayed higher QY (up to 80.8 ± 5.1%) compared with undoped CDs because boron-doping is conducive to changing the configuration of C and N bonding patterns, thereby increasing fraction of graphitic C and N and producing well-distributed surface defect states. Meanwhile, the BN-CDs contain fewer multi-chromophoric units compared with N-CDs with multiple emissive surface states after the addition of boron, causing the emission behaviour to change from excitation-dependent to near excitation-independent. Importantly, the authors showed that the longer lifetime of BN-CDs compared with N-CDs is due to the inhibition of a unique 5 ns component that arises from surface states in N-CDs (Fig. 7 ). In other words, the introduction of boron atoms in BN-CDs suppresses electron transitions of surface states due to the electron deficient nature of boron. Therefore, the high QY of BN-CDs is attributed to fluorescence dominated by highly emissive molecular state with long lifetime.

In addition, Yang’s group [153] found that green fluorescence from B-GQDs in the presence of a borax solution is stronger than that of B-GQDs in the absence of borax due to boron doping, as well as the interaction between B-GQDs and borax (Fig. 8 ). Because B3+ is linked to sp2 carbon clusters in GQDs, the short distance and energy level overlap can cause effective energy transfer from B3+ to the sp2 cluster, increasing the radiative recombination rate and enhancing the fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the electron deficient feature of B causes an interaction between B-GQDs and O in nearby Na2B4O7, to which the bright green fluorescence can be attributed. Moreover, Zboril’s group [69] prepared B-CDs from boric acid as the boron source through microwave heating. B-CDs have only a small negative surface charge (−10 mV), probably due to the presence of charged borate ester bridges [159]. Importantly, B-CDs exhibit significantly enhanced nonlinear optical response compared to undoped CDs.

Fluorine has maximum electronegativity, and fluorine moieties strongly absorb adjacent electrons and increase the separation of positive and negative charges. Fluorine doped CDs (F-CDs) remain rarely reported. Fluorine sources for preparation of F-CDs include 4,5-difluoro-1,2-benzenediamin, [160] fluorinated diglycidyl ethers, [60] tetrafluoroterephthalic acid, [161] and sodium fluoride [81].

Many reports on the optical properties and applications of silicon doped CDs (Si-CDs) have been published in recent years. Silicon belongs to the same group as C (group IV), but it strongly prefers sp3-like bonding and is considered to influence the electronic and structural properties of CDs in a different way compared to N doping. Common silicon sources are silicon tetrachloride (SiCl4) [163] and organosilanes, such as N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS) and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) [48,49,164–175].

The use of organosilane as a coordinating and passivation agent can improve the QY of CDs [173]. First, organosilane functional groups as surface passivation agents can introduce a new surface state. Second, organosilane functional groups as electron donors increase the electron cloud density, thus increasing the probability of electron-hole radiative recombination. Third, organosilane functional groups have a larger steric effect than O-related groups, which can accelerate π-π interaction between the carbon core and functional groups.

Benefiting from their unique merits such as high QY, stable solid-state fluorescence, and highly reactive silanol groups, Si-CDs can be used to fabricate Si-CDs-based films, glass, or monoliths. Liu et al. [169] prepared various Si-CDs gels, and the loading fraction of Si-CDs in the silica gel matrix could be easily tuned from 0 to 100%. Interesting, unlike dye-silica composites, the chemical bonding of Si-CDs to Gel glasses can enhance the QY and lifetime of Si-CDs because the silane groups with long chain structure completely prevent movement and aggregation (i.e. chemical dispersion on the molecular level) of Si-CDs with high fluorescence. The Si-CDs gel glasses can be easily cut into different shapes and sizes depending on the particular application, and they show long-term optical, thermal, and mechanical stabilities as well as high transmission in the visible and NIR regions. Next, Liu et al. [171] prepared Si-GQDs doped with a tuned concentration (0–100%) of organically modified silicate gel glasses with good processability (Fig. 13 ). Arbitrary doping in these gel glasses yields high transparency, good homogeneity, and full-UV shielding, thus these glasses are suitable for synthesis of bulk hybrid nanocomposites. Functional groups on the surface of Si-GQDs contribute to strong fluorescence both in solution (QY = 82%) and in the solid state (QY = 96%). The Si-GQDs and their gel glasses can absorb and transform UV light into blue light (Fig. 13), indicating that Si-GQDs embedded gel glass can be promising candidates for high performance UV filters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other non-metals dopants (iodine, chloride, selenium, and tellurium)
ce:section.ce:para: Other non-metallic elements such as iodine, chloride, selenium, and tellurium have been used to dope CDs, endowing CDs with unique properties.

Zhang et al. [176] prepared I-doped CDs (I-CDs) with excitation-dependent fluorescence using a hydrothermal method for the first time. A cube of high atomic number (Z = 53) endows iodine (I) with favorable X-ray attenuation property through photoelectric effect [177]. Therefore, these I-CDs exhibit superior X-ray attenuation and fluorescence, thus they could be used as efficient X-ray computed tomography contrast agents and fluorescence probes. I-CDs have the advantages of long circulation, they can distribute throughout the body after injection, and they can be excreted by renal clearance, thus they can be used in passive targeted computed tomography imaging.

Wang et al. [46] prepared chloride and nitrogen codoped CDs (Cl, N-CDs) using a choline chloride−glycerine deep eutectic solvent (DES) (Fig. 14 ). Cl, N-CDs exhibit a pH-sensitive behavior due to protonation and deprotonation of hydroxyl and amine groups on their surface. In addition, the Cl, N-CDs exhibit a fluorescent response towards cytochrome c with a linear range from 10 to 500 mg L−1 and were used for intracellular cytochrome c imaging.

Kang et al. [178] produced heavy doped CQDs with tunable fluorescence on a large scale through hydrothermal reduction. The authors demonstrated that fluorescent emission and QY of CQDs is closely related to the electronegativity of heteroatoms. Doping with S or Se can facilitate red-shifted emission compared to undoped CQDs (i.e., oxidized-CQDs), which is directly attributed to the low electronegativity of S and Se (Fig. 15 ), which act as an electron donor [179,180]. In contrast, nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibit blue-shifted fluorescent emission (Fig. 15). Furthermore, N acts as an electron acceptor when linked with the conjugation structure in CQDs, making charge transfer obvious and producing high QY [181,182]. Moreover, oxidized CQDs exhibit low luminescent efficiency despite the fact that oxygen is a strong electron acceptor because oxygen-related groups always cause nonradiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs and restrain emission from the intrinsic state [22,183,184]. In addition, selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) have longer fluorescence lifetime than that of oxidized CQDs, possibly due to inhibition of nonradiative energy transitions in Se-CQDs.

Tang’s group [185] developed tellurium-doped CDs (Te-CDs) using Te-containing molecular probes (FO-PTe) as the carbon source for sensitive detection of superoxide anion (O2 −). Te-CDs showed reversible detection of O2 − based on the redox properties of Te-centers on the surface of Te-CDs, producing the highest sensitivity with limit of detection as low as 8.0 pM. Finally, Te-CDs can be used for real-time, dynamic imaging of O2 − levels in the brain of mild depression mice.

Copper is a strong electron acceptor and donor, thus Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) have been used for various types of catalytic reactions. Moreover, copper in Cu-CDs can broaden and strengthen the absorption band compared to undoped CDs, indicating that Cu-CDs have stronger optical traps than undoped CDs [187]. Currently, copper sources used for preparing Cu-CDs are copper acetate monohydrate, [187] Na2[Cu(EDTA)], [53,80,91,186,188] cupric nitrate, [189] and cuprous chloride [190].

Cu-CDs have been widely used in photocatalytic reactions. Zeng’s group [80] found that copper doping can enhance the electron-accepting and donating abilities, producing Cu-to-graphite charge transfer absorption and improving the electric conductivity to 171.8 μs cm−1. In view of these advantages, the photocatalytic efficiency of Cu-CDs in photo-oxidation reactions involving 1,4-dihydro-2,6-dimethylpyridine-3,5-dicarboxylate improved by a factor 3.5 compared to that of undoped CDs. Furthermore, Shi’s group [186] proposed a model catalyst system by combining Cu-CDs and layered double hydroxides (LDHs). The confined nano-interlayer of anisotropic LDHs provides Cu-CDs with highly aligned dipoles and enhanced spin-orbit coupling, resulting in forbidden radiative recombination and prolonged exciton lifetime (Fig. 16A). The LDHs can also be used as nano-reactors, offering a confined microenvironment for catalysis, which shortens the electron transfer pathway and reduces exciton losses (Fig. 16B). Benefiting from these improved properties, the photocatalytic efficiency of Cu-CDs-LDHs in photo-oxidation reactions of 1,4-dihydro-2,6-dimethylpyridine-3,5-dicarboxylateis (1,4-DHP) is a factor 1.54 higher than that of Cu-CDs.

Importantly, Cu-CDs as catalysts can also be used for “click chemistry”. Our group [53] developed Cu(I)-doped CQDs [Cu(I)-CQDs] with zigzag structure for highly efficient catalysis in the Huisgen 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition reaction (Fig. 17 ). The ability to catalyze azide–alkyne cycloadditions of Cu(I)-CQDs was directly attributed to the controlled release of Cu(I) during exposure to UV light, enabling photo-induced “click chemistry”. Cu doping in Cu(I)-CQDs can form Cu–N bonds, producing Cu-to-graphite charge-transfer absorption and increasing the absorption intensity [191,192]. The fact that the energy level in Cu(I)-CQDs is far lower than that of 365 nm UV light allows free electrons to escape from excitons, thus releasing Cu(I) ions from Cu(I)-CQDs. In other words, when excited by 365 nm UV light, photoexcited holes can strongly compete with Cu(I) ions, [193] resulting in release of Cu(I) ions and allowing their further use for catalysing the Huisgen 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition reaction.

Gadolinium, a lanthanide element, is the most commonly used T1 contrast agents because Gd(III) affords even unpaired electron spins, which leads to high r1 relaxivities. Common Gd sources are gadolinium (III) chloride [50,51,194–197] and gadopentetic acid (GDPA) [199–203].

The Gd-doped CDs (Gd-CDs) have lower toxicity and improved stability, even in harsh biological environments, compared to other Gd-containing compounds. Therefore, Gd-CDs as a novel type of T1 contrast agent can be used for fluorescence and magnetic resonance (MR) dual-modal bioimaging. Xie et al. [199] reported the synthesis of Gd-CDs with QY of 19.7% by a simple carbonization of GDPA. The as-prepared Gd-CDs can act as dual functional probes for real-time MR and fluorescence imaging due to their high r1 relaxivity (5.88 mM−1 s−1) and stable fluorescence. Importantly, 12 nm Gd-CDs can be efficiently excreted through urine after injection and are minimally toxic. Their group [198] prepared Gd-CDs with different sizes using mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) as a template. The smallest Gd-CDs (3 nm) exhibited the highest r1 relaxivity (10 mM−1 s−1) and fluorescence QY (30.2%), and the Gd-CDs were further modified by c(RGDyK), a tumor-targeting ligand. After injecting into U87MG xenograft tumor, the c(RGDyK) modified Gd-CDs showed good tumor targeting ability (Fig. 18 ). Unbound Gd-CDs were efficiently excreted through renal clearance, preventing long-term toxicity.

Lanthanide ions have gained much attention due to their unique fluorescent properties, such as narrow emission band, large stokes shift, and long fluorescence lifetime. In addition to Gd, some other lanthanide elements such as terbium and europium ions have been used to prepare lanthanide-doped CDs [31,56,57,79,204,205].

Typical lanthanide ions such as ytterbium(III) and neodymium(III) have received a great deal of attention due to their characteristic NIR emission with relatively long fluorescence lifetime. Wong’s group [204] prepared lanthanide doped CQDs (Ln-CQDs) via one-pot hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid as a carbon source and either Yb3+ or Nd3+ as a dopant. Yb3+and Nd3+ occupy Ln-CQDs through lanthanide-oxygen bonding. Ln-CQDs exhibit characteristic NIR emission. Emission peaks centered near 998 and 1068 nm were ascribed to energy transfer from CQDs to lanthanide ions due to overlap between the emission spectrum of Ln-CQDs and the excitation spectrum of lanthanide ions (Fig. 19 ). In addition, the average lifetimes of Ln-CQDs exhibited faster decay than undoped CQDs. This is because the emission that arises from defect states exhibits a longer recombination lifetime than that from intrinsic states, and Ln-CQDs probably correspond to recombination from defect states [206]. As a result, Ln-CQDs can act as visible imaging probes and as antennae for fluorescence from lanthanide ions. Liu’s group [57] prepared Eu-doped CDs (Eu-CDs) using a facile microwave-assisted method. Fluorescence from Eu-CDs can be strongly affected by Eu content. Eu doping can improve the QY in CDs and red-shifts the fluorescence, indicating a large energy discrepancy.

Transition metals like manganese can be used to adjust the optical, electronic, and magnetic properties of Mn-doped nanoparticles [207]. A new sub-band can form when Mn(II) is introduced into the core of nanoparticles with rational arrangement that partially blocks the charge recombination pathway at oxygen-related surface traps [208]. As a result, it increases the excitons by intrinsic band radiation and causes the Mn(II)-induced functionalization of nanoparticles [208–210].

In addition, Mn(II) doping in CDs also allows these CDs to be used for MR imaging. Xu et al. [211] prepared Mn-CDs with high r1 relaxivity of 3.26 ± 0.11 mM−1 s−1 and high fluorescence QY of 90.76% through a one-step solvothermal method. The T1 relaxation time decreased and the T1-weighted MR image became brighter as the Mn content in Mn-CDs increased. Mn doping can be used to enhance the luminescence efficiency from CDs because Mn(II) influences the electron distribution in CDs particles and in turn enhances their fluorescence. As a result, Mn-CDs can act as MRI/fluorescence bifunctional imaging probes for accurate diagnosis after modifying the targeting molecule anti-HE4 mAb (Fig. 22 ). Li et al. [54] obtained Mn-CDs with fluorescence and MR dual-mode imaging by solvothermal synthesis. The QY in these Mn-CDs increased from 2.27% to 6.75% as the content of Mn(II) increased, and these Mn-CDs showed high r1 relaxivity (7.28 mM−1 s−1). Mn(II)-doping can influence the energy levels of the sp2 core in the CDs, shifting their emission-wavelength. When the Mn-CDs were excited, Mn(II) absorbs photons via 6A1→4T1 transitions, followed by energy transfer between Mn(II) and the sp2 core. Radiative recombination in the sp2 core energy levels enhanced the fluorescence [212].

Zinc is an important element that assists the electron-transfer process, is an essential trace element in biological systems, and is a promising metal dopant for CDs. The introduction of zinc was shown to enhance various properties, such photocatalytic activity [213] and performance as a photodetector [214]. According to current research, zinc exists in zinc-doped CDs (Zn-CDs) as ZnO. Zn-CDs usually show excellent photocatalytic properties due to the wide band gap (3.37 eV) of ZnO. Importantly, a clear overlap was observed between the fluorescence range of ZnO and CDs, which facilitates charge/energy transfer between ZnO and CDs [59].

Metal elements can act as electron donors in CDs, promoting electron transfer and introducing new functionalities in CDs. Zinc doping can efficiently improve the QY in CDs due to heteroatom directed, oxidized carbon-based surface passivation, which stabilizes the excited state in CDs [59,135] Li et al. [59] prepared Zn-CDs with high QY (32.3%) through one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Zn-CDs are highly monodisperse and exhibit excitation-independent fluorescence. The authors showed that enhanced QY in the Zn-CDs results from improved electron or hole purging. This purging arises from a shift in the energy states in Zn-CDs as ionic Zn binds to CDs. In addition, Zn-CDs can act as Fenton reaction-based bio-sensors for detecting hydrogen peroxide and glucose. Very recently, Xu et al. [216] prepared Zn-CDs with QY reaching 35% and showed that Zn2+ acts as a surface passivation agent, preventing aggregation of graphene π–π stacking, forming sp3/sp2 hybrid hybridization, and increasing the QY in CDs. Moreover, Zn doping can increase the fluorescence lifetime of CDs by stabilizing their excited-state.

Moreover, Zn-CDs exhibit photocatalytic activity with high efficiency. Sonkar’s group [215] prepared bright fluorescent Zn-CDs with red excitation-independent emission. Surface doping of Zn improved the QY and can be used for photoreduction of hexavalent chromium(VI) to trivalent chromium(III) in sunlight (Fig. 23 ). The increased photoreduction efficiency of Zn-CDs could be attributed to the high catalytic activity of ZnO, which facilitates reduction and recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [217]. Moreover, another plausible reason for the high photocatalytic activity of Zn-CDs for chromium(VI) reduction is ascribed to their high adsorption values. On the other hand, the solubility is enhanced due to the presence of defects in Zn-CDs, which plays an important role in photoreduction as chromium(VI) approaches active sites on the Zn-CDs surface and undergoes redox reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other metal dopants (Ge, Mg, Co, Ru and Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, some other metal dopants are used to synthesize of metal-doped CDs, such as Ge, Mg, Co, Ru, and Fe elements.

Germanium, a member of group IV, is more inclined to sp3-like bonding and is considered to influence the electronic and structural properties of carbon-based materials, albeit in a different manner compared with N or S doping. Our group [58] prepared germanium doped CDs (Ge-CDs) with blue fluorescence via a one-step carbonation method (Fig. 24 ). Germanium doping can improve the QY of CDs as germanium influences surface-trapped electrons and the structural properties of CDs. In addition, Ge-CDs exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence that is attributed to

a single transition mode [13]. Moreover, fluorescence from Ge-CDs may be attributed to radiative recombination of surface-trapped electrons and holes [218,219]. Importantly, the as-prepared Ge-CDs could be applied for detection of Hg2+ based on charge transfer quenching at concentration ranging from 0.2 to 3 μM with a limit of detection of approximately 75 nM.

Metal chelates show superior thermal stability for their chelate rings, which are similar to aromatic rings, thus the amino and carboxyl groups can be protected by a chelating effect during dehydration and carbonization. Tian et al. [104] prepared Mg/N co-doped CDs (Mg, N-CDs) with QY reaching 83% through a one-step hydrothermal method. The QY of Mg, N-CDs is obviously higher than that of CDs without any doping as well as N-CDs because the Mg-citric acid chelate introduces Mg atom into CDs and preserves the carboxyl group; both effects intensify the fluorescence of Mg, N-CDs. Li et al. [61] developed Mg, N-CQDs with blue fluorescence using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and Mg(OH)2 as precursors (Fig. 25A). Similarly, Mg/N codoping can efficiently enhance the QY in CQDs. The high QY (58.8%) of Mg, N-CQDs can be attributed to radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs and charge transfer from a surface-doped heteroatom to carbon [23,220]. In addition, amino/carboxyl groups can be protected by Mg(OH)2 through a chelating effect. Furthermore, the Mg, N-CQDs as a fluorescent probe can be used for detecting Hg(II) and Cys at concentration ranging from 0.05 to 5 μM and from 9 to 15 μM, respectively (Fig. 25B).

Co, N-codoped nanoparticles are proven to be potential non-precious catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), and the Co and N concentration can influence the electrocatalytic activity. Kang’s group [221] produced cobalt-doped CQDs (Co-CQDs) via facile pyrolysis of vitamin B12 (VB12) and CQDs. These Co-CQDs contained a porous structure and well-controlled concentrations of Co and N, which are beneficial for improving the electrocatalytic activity of Co-CQDs. In particular, Co-CQDs containing 1.12% Co and 2.92% N synthesized at 700 °C (Co1.12/N2.92-700) showed the best electrocatalytic activity and selectivity for ORR through a four-electron pathway (Fig. 26 ). Moreover, Feng’s group [222] prepared cobalt-doped CDs (Co-CDs) via a one-pot solvothermal route. Interesting, Co-CDs show a distinctive IPFRET system from surface state emission to Co-related emission, thus reducing the emission intensity from Co-CDs.

Basu et al. [86] prepared ruthenium-doped CDs (Ru-CDs) by pyrolyzing citric acid and RuCl3, and they further discussed the origin of fluorescence. Ruthenium doping can improve the fluorescent QY of CDs because electron hopping rate from the carbon core to the surface reaches is maximized at a certain dopant concentration, which increases the QY in the doped material. Ruthenium may provide effective surface passivation when combined with deferent arrays of π-electrons present in CDs, in turn causing excitation-dependent fluorescence. On the other hand, excitation-dependent behavior is also caused by a combination of a dynamically hydrogen-bonded alcohol on chromophores with collective proton motion and excited HO···C = O (n−π*) interactions, which can be regarded as a Burgi−Dunitz trajectory interaction [223]. Modification of Ru-CDs with EDA can form Ru-CDs-EDA, which exhibits excitation-independent emission. This is because EDA can react with the αβ-unsaturated ketone in Ru-CDs by Michael addition [224,225], which reduces multiple types of conjugations to a single conjugation.

Iron doping can endow CDs with superior catalytic properties. Iron-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) can be used for ORR due to their strong catalytic capability, which arises from coordination of iron with nitrogen doping in carbon. Wei et al. [62] reported Fe and N co-doped CDs (Fe, N-CDs) for the first time, which were prepared through a facile microwave solvothermal method. Fe doping in Fe, N-CDs gives rise to intrinsic peroxidase activity through catalyzed oxidation of 3, 3, 5, 5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a deeper color in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 27 ). Compared with natural horseradish peroxidase, Fe, N-CDs showed stable catalytic performance either over a wider pH range or under at varying temperature. A colorimetric method for optical H2O2 detection was developed by using Fe, N-CDs as a catalyst.

Surface modification is a strategy for functionalizing CDs with goal orientation. Due to abundant surface groups on CDs, the functional ligand can readily bind on the surface of CDs through special interactions, such as coordination, amidation reaction, and electrostatic interactions. Currently, various functional ligands have been used for surface modification of CDs, including ions, organic molecules, polymers, DNA, and proteins. Surface modification through the use of a functional ligand is usually complex and tedious compared to facile and direct heteroatom doping, but it can provide CDs with other unique properties. Meanwhile, surface modification is also a very efficient method for further improving the QY after the formation of CDs, i.e., surface passivation.

Cations and anions, especially metal ions, can be easily linked to the surface of CDs for introducing new active sites, thus providing a means for emerging chemical reactivities and potential applications.
Some common transition metal ions such as Fe3+, Cu2+, and Mn2+ have been used to modify CDs due to their unique photophysical and chemical properties [207,226–229].

Iron(III) chloride is a mild oxidizing agent that can remove H atoms from an aromatic substrate, resulting in new carbon-carbon bonds between adjacent rings. Using iron(III) to modify the surface of CDs can improve the catalytic properties of CDs. Cu(II) ions associated with CDs add important biocatalytic functions, thus providing a means for catalyzing various chemical reactions. Zboril’s group [227] synthesized Fe(III) modified CDs (CDs-Fe) by oxidative coupling of xylene molecules with anhydrous FeCl3 (Fig. 28 ). These CDs-Fe showed superior catalytic activity for hydrogenation of alkenes with excellent conversion and selectivity (up to 100%). CDs-Fe as heterogeneous catalysts can be used repeatedly without any loss of catalytic efficiency, thus they are also more effective in hydrogenation of various electron donating or withdrawing alkenes molecules compared to other Fe(III)-based catalysts. Moreover, the catalytic stability of CDs-Fe can be monitored by measuring the fluorescence intensity or QY. Willner et al. [226] prepared Cu(II)-modified CDs (CDs-Cu) as catalysts for catalyzing oxidation of dopamine by H2O2 to form aminochrome. Furthermore, amino-β-cyclodextrin can be linked to the surface of CDs-Cu through amidation, leading to the formation of amino-β-cyclodextrin-modified CDs-Cu. The introduction of amino-β-cyclodextrin can cause a 4-fold enhancement in catalytic oxidation of dopamine to produce aminochrome because binding of dopamine to amino-β-cyclodextrin receptor sites should promote local concentration of dopamine at the heterogeneous catalyst. Moreover, the abundant and diverse surface groups that can be used to functionalize CDs gives these CDs good binding ability toward various metal ions, thus these CDs have a potential use in multifunctional sensor arrays.

Lanthanide ions such as Tb3+ and Eu3+ have been used to modify CDs [181,182,230–235]. Lanthanide ions usually have a certain affinity to oxygen-donor atoms, thus they can coordinate with carboxylate groups, acting as a bridge to link neighboring carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs [236]. Lanthanide ions have low luminescent efficiency but can emit strong fluorescence due to the antenna effect when specifically integrated with ligands [237]. Therefore, CDs modified with lanthanide ions could be used in fluorescent ratiometry nanoprobes.

Lanthanide ions can link on the surface of CDs via Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and lanthanide ions. Feldmann et al. [235] prepared Tb3+/Eu3+-modified CDs (CDs-Tb/CDs-Eu) to study the optical properties of lanthanide ions modified CDs. An FRET process from CDs to Tb3+/Eu3+ can occur when excited at a certain energy. As a close distance (<4 Å) between corresponding luminescent centers is essential for efficient FRET, only lanthanide ions coordinated by polyol functionalization directly on the surface of CDs can serve as active emitters (Fig. 29a). Excitation of CDs-Tb and CDs-Eu indeed results in intense characteristics, line-type f→f transitions at high intensity, and excellent QY of 85% for CDs-Tb and 75% for CDs-Eu (Fig. 29b and c). Whereas certain emission from CDs is still visible at 350–550 nm, 275 nm excitation of CDs-Tb only produces Tb3+-related emission, indicating a very efficient FRET process (Fig. 29b).

Modification with lanthanide ions can change the fluorescent properties of CDs and illustrates the potential for CDs to be used in ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes. Tang et al. [231] prepared CDs-Tb for ratiometric fluorescent detection of an anthrax biomarker, dipicolinic acid (DPA). DPA enhances the emission intensity from Tb3+, which is ascribed to efficient energy transfer from DPA ligands to Tb3+, but the DPA does not affect the fluorescence from CDs. Therefore, CDs-Tb nanoprobe exhibits a linear relationship at concentration ranging from 0.005 to 1.2 μM.

Ionic self-assembly exploits cooperative charge interactions to provide a very efficient and simple noncovalent method for constructing soft materials. Hao’s group [232] developed a simple method for constructing vesicles via hybrid self-assembly of unmodified DNA and Ce3+ modified CQDs (CQDs-Ce) (Fig. 30 ). These CQDs-Ce provided fluorescence and self-assembly unit, where DNA molecules act as rigid biomolecular scaffolds. The formation of vesicles is a synergetic result of coordination between Ce3+ and carbonyl groups in CQDs, electrostatic interactions between DNA and CQDs-Ce, and hydrophobic forces between adjacent CQDs-Ce (Fig. 30). Moreover, self-assembly of DNA and CQDs-Ce is accompanied by a weakening of the fluorescence due to aggregation of CQDs-Ce with DNA, resulting in nonradiative energy transfer.

Polymers have been widely used as excellent surface passivation agents for modifying the surface and improving the QY of CDs. Common polymers are PEG1500N, [29,218,238] PPEI-EI, [239] polyethylenimine(PEI), [64,240–242] poly(diphenylbutadiyne) (PDPB), [243] a four-arm star polymer (THPD), [244] polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), [245] PEG200, [246] polydopamine, [247] and poly(ethylenimine) [248].

Polymer modification is a very efficient approach for enhancing the luminescence efficiency of CDs, i.e., via surface passivation. Sun et al. [29] clearly showed that CDs exhibit increased fluorescence in a solution-like suspension and in the solid state after the CD surface was treated with PEG1500N because surface passivation can stabilize emission from surface energy traps. Note that emissive energy traps must remain bound to the particle surface via quantum confinement, i.e., the particle must have a large surface-to-volume ratio if surface passivation is to produce strong fluorescence.

PEG is a hydrophilic linear polymer that can be used for protein and peptide modification to improve solubility, reduce toxicity, and extend the circulation half-life. Thus, modification of CDs with PEG has potential applications in drug delivery systems for improving the biocompatibility and prolonging the circulation time of CDs in vivo. In addition, the addition of polymers on the surface of CDs can be used to tune and improve their optical properties. Chang et al. [243] prepared PDPB-modified CDs (CDs-PDPB) through a facile solid reaction without the need for a template, solvent, or surfactant. Importantly, the incorporation of CDs into the PDPB polymer significantly improved visible light absorption and enhanced energy conversion, thus improving the photodegradation performance. Yan et al. [244] developed nanostructured light-harvesting antennas (LHAs) by exploiting the positively charged THPD as a core for framing electrostatic self-assembly of negatively charged GQDs on the surface. This nanosystem provides high energy transfer efficiency reaching 93.6% and an antenna effect of 7.3 in aqueous solution. The PEI is a cationic polymer that has been used for surface modification of graphene nanomaterials and linking of two nanoconstructs or moieties. Srivastava’ group [249] developed a stable complex of GO and GQDs by an electrostatic layer-by-layer assembly via a polyethylene imine bridge for cell imaging, photothermal therapy, and oxidative stress response in breast cancer cells. This complex showed an excellent photothermal response (44–49 °C) upon 808 nm laser (0.5 W cm−2) exposure for 5 min.

Furthermore, the introduction of polymers can produce new active sites on the surface of CDs, providing these CDs with more potential applications. Our group [240] described a novel fluorescence method for sensing N-acetylcysteine (NAC) using a fluorescent off–on binary system (Fig. 31 ). The amino groups of PEI-modified CDs (CDs-PEI) can bind to Cu2+ to form surface cupric amine complexes, resulting in strong fluorescence quenching of CDs-PEI via electron transfer between Cu2+ complexes and CDs-PEI. But the electron transfer take place from mercap to group on NAC to Cu2+ in the presence of NAC, causing the formation of Cu+ and the dissociation of cupric amine complexes, thereby turning on the fluorescence of the system. This method showed a good linear relationship towards NAC with concentration ranging from 5.56 to 277.8 μM with detection limit of 0.56 μM.

Currently, many small organic molecules have been reported for modifying the surface of CDs, including hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), [250,251] tyramine, [252] L-cysteine, [27] folic acid, [253] D/L-penicillamine, [254] cyclam, [255] carboxymethyl-β-cyclodextrin, [256] triphenylphosphonium (TPP), [257–259] and ethylenediamine (EDA) [260].

CDs modified with small organic molecules can be used to adjust the fluorescence and enhance the QY in CDs through surface passivation. Song’s group [251] proposed an effective method for modulating the radiative transitions in CDs by introducing CTAB. The introduction of CTAB may eliminate surface defects in CDs. Moreover, the increased QY and decay times can be ascribed to suppressed nonradiative electronic transitions at surface states (Fig. 32a and b). Some organic groups such as carboxyl groups linked on the surface of CDs act as nonradiative traps that can quench fluorescence from localized electron–hole pairs. After modification with CTAB, the carboxyl groups partially reacted with CTAB, leading to interactions between [–COO−] and [CH3(CH2)12N+(CH3)3] and increasing the intensity of green and red emission. Meanwhile, CTAB may prevent self-quenching or emission re-absorption in CDs because the CTAB chain can efficiently isolate CDs and improve the QY. Furthermore, CDs-based solid-state films were prepared by dispersing CDs in a PVP matrix, where solid-state quenching was effectively avoided. This arises due to energy transfer from sp2 domains to surface-related states because vibration and rotation of surface organic groups is restricted by the immobilization and homogeneous dispersion of CDs in the PVP matrix (Fig. 32c).

Some small molecules with special targeting ability modified on the surface of CDs can provide CDs with their unique properties. Wu’s group [259] constructed a nanosystem with mitochondria-targeted and photoresponsive NO-releasing properties for cancer treatment. This nanosystem was built by covalently incorporating a NO photo-donor and a mitochondria targeting molecule onto the surface of CDs (Fig. 33 ). This nanosystem contained immobilized S-nitrosothiol (a typical NO donor) that can release NO continuously for at least 8 h. The nanosystem can target mitochondria and release NO after entering cancer cells, which can cause high cytotoxicity by specifically damaging their mitochondria. In addition, irradiation with light can increase the release rate of NO, boosting cell apoptosis. Therefore, the modification of mitochondria-targeting molecules on the surface of CDs can improve its pro-apoptosis action.

The development of highly sensitive assays for enzyme activity and inhibitor screening is of great significance for further understanding their function and the early diagnosis of diseases. Feng’ group [261] developed a sensitive detection strategy for β-glycosidase activity and inhibitor screening by combining the host-guest recognition and static quenching mechanism between β-cyclodextrin modified CQDs and p-nitrophenol. Furthermore, this CQDs was also used for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of cholesterol based on the competitive host-guest recognition [262]. This method showed good analytical performance in the detection of cholesterol with lowest detection limit of 0.7 ± 0.1 μM. Moreover, Ma’ group [263] prepared dual-labeled CDs by modifying pH-sensitive fluoresceinisothiocyanate and pH-insensitive rhodamine B isothiocyanate on the surface of amino-coated CDs. This dual-labeled CDs as a tunable ratiometric fluorescent pH nanosensor can be applied for quantitatively monitoring the intracellular pH fluctuations.

Das et al. [264] developed a facile strategy for self-assembly of CDs and ssDNA through conjugating amine modified CDs with the terminal phosphate of ssDNA. Hybridization-mediated self-assembly of CDs-ssDNA conjugates (S1)2-CDs and its complementary (S2)2-CDs, resulting in the creation of a CDs array [(S1)2-CDs-(S2)2-CDs]n (Fig. 34 ). Varying the DNA sequence length can be used to adjust the distance between adjacent CDs. The CDs-ssDNA array exhibited increased fluorescence that is directly attributed to DNA self-assembly, which prevents aggregation of CDs. Hu’s group [265] prepared CDs with green-yellow fluorescence via a one-pot low-temperature carbonization method. The as-obtained CDs are characterized by small size, high QY, good dispersibility, and excitation-dependent emission. Importantly, these CDs can intercalate into the base pairs of calf thymus DNA (ctDNA). The fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased with the addition of ctDNA.

The nuclear localization signal (NLS) peptide is a short amino acid sequence that can deliver cargo proteins into the cell nucleus through the nuclear pore complex. On this basis, Yang’s group [266] prepared PEG-modified CDs with blue fluorescence through a one-step hydrothermal method. The NLS-modified CDs with the ability to target a cell nucleus were further obtained through an EDC/NHS coupling reaction. The NLS-modified CDs can pass through the nuclear pore, enter the cell nucleus, and subsequently localize in the cell nucleus (Fig. 35 ). The cell uptake of CDs occurs mainly through endocytosis mechanism. The endocytosis pathway can be divided into two major pathways: phagocytosis and pinocytosis. The former involves uptake of relatively larger particles, while the latter deals with the uptake of fluids and relatively smaller particles such as CDs. Srivastava’ group [103] demonstrated that the primary mechanism of cell uptake is an active process (energy-dependent pathway). The “self-assembled” GQDs have not been labelled with nuclear localizing signals or proteins, but can achieve targeting imaging of nucleus via caveolae and clathrin-mediated endocytosis.

In this review, we introduced in depth and in detail the current progress on functionalization of CDs, including heteroatom doping and surface modification. These two functionalization strategies can be used to adjust the physical, chemical, optical, and electrical properties of CDs, extending their application range.

Heteroatom doping and surface modification can be used to efficiently adjust the intrinsic structure and surface state, subsequently changing the physical and optical properties of CDs. However, it is still very difficult to explain how functionalization changes the intrinsic structural and luminescence mechanism due to the lack of clear analysis of the CDs structure. Generally, CDs are composed of a carbon-core and surface domains. The carbon-core domain is characterized by an sp2 graphene structure, whereas the surface domain contains abundant functional groups. These two domains are primarily responsible for fluorescence from CDs. In other words, fluorescence from CDs is closely related to their intrinsic structure and surface components. Among the current opinions, the quantum confinement effect, conjugated structures, and free zigzag sites can be used to explain the various fluorescence characteristics, which is determined by the carbon core. Meanwhile the surface state and edge defects can be used to explain fluorescence characteristics that are determined by hybridization of the carbon backbone and connected chemical groups simultaneously.

Although numerous efforts have focused on improving the functionality of CDs, several limitations still exist, hampering the use of CDs in certain research fields. For chemo and biosensing, it is a difficult challenge to enhance sensitivity while maintaining selectivity. In order to improve sensitivity and selectivity simultaneously, it is necessary to select appropriate precursors that determine the chemical properties of CDs. In addition, modification of the surface of CDs with special functional ligands is one promising strategy for developing functional nanoprobes for various corresponding targets. Second, except for fluorescence activation and deactivation, the phenomena of lifetime variation and spectra shift are expected to be used in CDs-based sensing in the near future. Regarding bioimaging and biomedicine, it would be very useful if CDs exhibit absorption and fluorescence in the NIR region in order to avoid auto-fluorescence from cells and tissues. Optoelectronic applications require CDs with high color purity (i.e., narrow line width) luminescence and warm colors, such as amber and red, by tuning of their electronic structures. There is no doubt that CDs will complement the weak points of organic fluorophores and inorganic nanocrystals, guaranteeing themselves a bright future.
